title: Indigenous Diplomacy and Engagement Act
plain_summary: This law creates a new government office to help the United States work better with Indigenous people from other countries around the world. The office will be run by a special coordinator who will make plans and make sure all parts of the government work together on Indigenous issues. The law also sets up a group of advisors to give suggestions and requires the government to write reports to Congress about how this work is going. This will help the United States have better relationships with Indigenous communities in other nations.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Indigenous Diplomacy and Engagement Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. There are about four hundred seventy six million Indigenous people around the world. These people share a common history of having ancestral connections to the lands and natural resources where they and their ancestors have lived. They also have their own unique cultures, languages, and traditions. Indigenous people around the world share the common challenge and goal of preserving and protecting their ancestral lands, natural resources, cultures, languages, and traditions. The rights, challenges, and goals of Indigenous people worldwide have been recognized by international organizations. The United Nations recognized these rights in its Declaration of the Rights of Indigenous Peoples on September thirteenth two thousand seven. The United States should expand its direct work with Indigenous people worldwide to better help them achieve their goals and focus on common challenges and opportunities.

sections.title: Section 3  Comprehensive International Strategy for Promoting Diplomacy and Engagement with Indigenous Peoples
sections.text: The President must develop and carry out a complete five year international plan for promoting diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries. This must happen no later than one year after this law is passed, and then every five years after that. The President will work through a Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs who will be appointed under section four of this law. The President must also submit this plan to the appropriate congressional committees. The plan must include several parts. First, it must identify at least ten but no more than twenty countries that are geographically, ethnically, and culturally diverse and have significant populations of Indigenous people. Second, it must assess several things about each country identified. This includes looking at how much the United States currently works with Indigenous people in that country, how much that country's government promotes exchanges between Indigenous people from different countries, opportunities for the United States to start or improve diplomacy with Indigenous people in that country and how this would support broader foreign policy goals of the United States, and ways to measure the overall progress of United States efforts to promote and help diplomacy with Indigenous people in that country. Third, the plan must include a detailed outline of specific programs, activities, or other efforts that may be done. This should promote and help activities that enhance diplomacy and engagement with Indigenous people in each country, working together with that country's government. It should also coordinate with existing programs involving Indigenous people, including working with heads of nongovernmental organizations, civil society, and Indigenous groups. Fourth, the plan must identify the general levels of resources needed each year to implement the plan in each country, taking into account activities and funding provided by the country and multilateral institutions and using private sector resources. Fifth, it must identify which Federal departments and agencies will conduct the programs, activities, and other efforts described in the plan. Sixth, for each Federal department or agency, it must assess the expected contributions of that department or agency to implement the plan, including technical, financial, and in-kind contributions. When developing this plan, the President, working through the Coordinator, must consult with several people and groups. These include the Secretary of State, the Secretary of the Interior, the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development, the Chief Executive Officer of the Millennium Challenge Corporation, the Chief Executive Officer of the Development Finance Corporation, the Advisory Commission on Indigenous Peoples established under section six, and nongovernmental organizations that have sufficient background, knowledge, and expertise working on Indigenous rights or resources issues or engaging with Indigenous people from other countries. When implementing the plan, the President must make sure that the policies and initiatives carried out are designed to achieve maximum impact and long-term sustainability.

sections.title: Section 4  Office for Indigenous Affairs
sections.text: This section creates an Office for Indigenous Affairs to coordinate all efforts of the Federal Government regarding diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries. The Office will be headed by the Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs. The President will appoint this person with the advice and consent of the Senate. The Coordinator will report directly to the Secretary of State and will have the rank and status of Ambassador at Large. The Coordinator has several responsibilities. First, to design, oversee, and coordinate activities and programs of the Federal Government relating to diplomacy and engagement with Indigenous people from other countries. Second, to direct United States resources to increase and enhance diplomatic exchange and working with Indigenous people from other countries and to assist in developing the comprehensive international strategy under section three to promote diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries and Indigenous people in the United States. Third, to coordinate all policies, programs, and funding relating to diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries of relevant United States Government agencies, including the Department of State, the United States Agency for International Development, the Department of the Interior, the Millennium Challenge Corporation, and the Development Finance Corporation. Fourth, to design, integrate, and as appropriate, implement policies, programs, and activities related to Indigenous health, education, economic development, Indigenous human rights from other countries, and protection of Indigenous people from other countries in humanitarian crises. Fifth, to encourage the Secretary of the Interior and the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development to establish department or agency specific guidelines regarding diplomacy and working between Indigenous people from other countries and Indigenous people in the United States and to monitor the implementation of those guidelines. In addition to these duties, the Coordinator will serve as the principal advisor to the Secretary of State regarding matters relating to Indigenous people from other countries. Subject to the direction of the President and the Secretary of State, the Coordinator may represent the United States in matters relevant to diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries. This includes contacts with Tribal representatives in other countries, foreign governments, nongovernmental organizations, the United Nations and its specialized agencies, and other international organizations of which the United States is a member. It also includes multilateral conferences and meetings relevant to diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries. There is authorized to be appropriated to the Coordinator such sums as may be necessary to carry out the duties and responsibilities of the Office for Indigenous Affairs. Amounts appropriated pursuant to this authorization are authorized to remain available until expended and shall supplement, not replace, amounts otherwise authorized to be appropriated to carry out the purposes described in this section.

sections.title: Section 5  Annual Report on Promoting International Diplomacy and Engagement with Indigenous People
sections.text: The Secretary of State, working through the Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs, must submit a report called the Report on International Indigenous Diplomacy and Engagement to the appropriate congressional committees. This must happen no later than one year after the date of submission of the comprehensive international strategy developed under section three, and then every four years after that. Each report must include several things. First, the goals and objectives of the comprehensive international strategy developed under section three. Second, the Coordinator's assessment of how effective the strategy has been so far, including the specific criteria used in that assessment. Third, a description of how all Federal Government resources and international activities to engage with and support Indigenous people from other countries are coordinated. This includes identifying the Federal departments or agencies involved, describing the coordination between the heads of those Federal departments and agencies, and describing the coordination with non Federal Government entities, including foreign governments, multilateral organizations and institutions, and nongovernmental organizations. Fourth, a description of the relationship between efforts to support Indigenous people from other countries, other United States assistance strategies in developing countries, and diplomatic relationships with those countries. Fifth, a description of efforts to design, support, and implement activities in response to the needs of Indigenous people from other countries and in furtherance of United States diplomatic and peacemaking initiatives. Sixth, a description of data collection efforts conducted under programs authorized by this Act or the amendments made by this Act. Seventh, a description of any significant efforts by bilateral or multilateral donors in support of Indigenous people from other countries. Eighth, an identification of all contractors, subcontractors, grantees, and subgrantees receiving United States funds for programs supporting Indigenous people from other countries. Ninth, recommendations related to best practices, effective strategies, and suggested improvements to enhance the impact of efforts to support Indigenous people from other countries. Tenth, a description of United States resources that are being used to implement the strategy developed under section three, to respond to needs of Indigenous people from other countries, to promote equity and advance the status of Indigenous people from other countries, and to encourage all parties to engage with and support the participation of Indigenous people from other countries in decisions affecting their lands, cultures, and traditions, the allocation of natural resources, and the protection of the civil and political rights of such peoples.

sections.title: Section 6  Advisory Commission on Indigenous Peoples
sections.text: This section creates an Advisory Commission on Indigenous Peoples within the Department of State. The Advisory Commission will be made up of several members. The Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs appointed under section four will serve as chair. Eight members will be appointed by the Secretary of State from among individuals who are not officers or employees of the Federal Government. Three members will be appointed by the President pro tempore of the Senate on the joint recommendation of the Majority and Minority Leaders of the Senate. Three members will be appointed by the Speaker of the House of Representatives on the joint recommendation of the Majority and Minority Leaders of the House of Representatives. One representative each from the National Congress of American Indians, the Alaska Federation of Natives, and the Office of Hawaiian Affairs. The members appointed by the Secretary of State, Senate, and House should be selected from academics who represent various scholarly approaches to the history of the rights and resources of Indigenous people from other countries, representatives of nongovernmental organizations and other institutions having the background, knowledge, and expertise related to diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries and Indigenous people in the United States, and other distinguished individuals noted for their diverse background, knowledge, and expertise in fields relevant to Indigenous people from other countries, including foreign affairs, human rights, and international law. These appointments should be made no later than one hundred twenty days after this law is passed. The term of each appointed member will be three years. Members can be reappointed for a second term. The Advisory Commission has several duties. First, it must annually make recommendations to the Secretary of State regarding best practices to promote diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries and the effective integration of such practices into the foreign policy of the United States, including assistance programming. Second, it must consult with members of the Federal Government, Indigenous leaders from the United States and other countries, and with private groups and individuals with expertise on promoting diplomacy and working with Indigenous people from other countries. In carrying out this section, the Advisory Commission may conduct hearings, sit at times and places, take testimony, and receive evidence, as the Advisory Commission considers appropriate. Members of the Advisory Commission will be allowed travel expenses, including per diem in lieu of subsistence at rates authorized for employees of agencies under subchapter I of chapter fifty seven of title five, United States Code, while away from their homes or regular places of business in the performance of duties for the Advisory Commission. No later than April first of each year, the Advisory Commission must submit to the President, the Secretary of State, and the appropriate congressional committees a report that sets forth its findings and recommendations for United States policy and programs.

sections.title: Section 7  Training for Foreign Service Officers on International Indigenous Diplomacy and Engagement
sections.text: This section changes the Foreign Service Act of nineteen eighty by adding new training requirements. The Secretary of State, working through the Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs, must establish training as part of the standard training provided for chiefs of mission, deputy chiefs of mission, and other officers of the Service before they leave for posting outside the United States. This training must cover the history and culture of the Indigenous communities that live near the applicable posts of assignment, along with guidance on ways to engage with and provide direct support to individuals from those communities.

sections.title: Section 8  Definitions
sections.text: This section defines important terms used in this Act. Appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Natural Resources of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Foreign Relations and the Committee on Indian Affairs of the Senate. Coordinator means the Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs appointed under section four. Domestic Indigenous peoples means Indians, as that term is defined in section four of the Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act, Native Hawaiians, as that term is defined in section six thousand two hundred seven of the Native Hawaiian Education Act, Alaska Natives, within the meaning provided for the term Native in section three of the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, and Pacific Islanders, as such term is defined in section eight hundred fifteen of the Native American Programs Act of nineteen seventy four. Indigenous peoples means distinct social and cultural groups that the Coordinator for Indigenous Affairs designates as such, taking into account relevant factors such as internationally recognized definitions of Indigenous Peoples, historical context, and sociological circumstances. This includes domestic Indigenous peoples and international Indigenous peoples. International Indigenous peoples means the peoples indigenous to foreign countries or foreign territories.
